1: The second full wasf (Arabic: descriptive praise poem) of the Song, unique in running from feet to head — an ascending rather than descending survey. This reversal of the usual head-to-foot sequence (used in Song of Solomon 4:1-7) is unparalleled in the surviving ANE love poetry corpus and may signal a new vantage point: the beloved is viewed in the context of a processional dance (machola ha-machanayim, the dance of the two camps, Song of Solomon 6:13). The title hassulammit — the only proper name for the beloved in the Song — derives most likely from Shlomo (Solomon) as its feminine counterpart, or possibly from shalom (peace/wholeness), making her 'the peaceable one.' The thighs compared to jewels worked by a craftsman: in the ancient Near East, skilled craftsmen (ben-aman, son of a master, a technical guild title) produced ornamental metalwork worn as identity markers. The quality of sandals was a social indicator: only slaves went unshod (cf. the sandal-binding idiom in John 1:27; Mark 1:7), making sandaled feet a marker of free status and honor.
2: The shorer (H8326, navel/umbilical region, a hapax legomenon appearing only here) contributes to the challenge of this verse: it is either the navel itself or the curve of the abdomen. The comparison to an 'aggan (H101, a mixing bowl for wine) — round and never lacking mixed wine — combines the visual image of a curved vessel with the sensory image of abundance. The belly like a heap of wheat (aremah hittim) surrounded by lilies is a landscape-to-body metaphor that appears frequently in Egyptian love poetry: the Papyrus Chester Beatty I (13th century BCE) parallels descriptions of the beloved's body using agricultural abundance imagery. The heap of wheat specifically recalls the threshing floor, the site of harvest abundance and — in biblical narrative — significant social encounters (Ruth 3; cf. also Hosea 9:1). The lily-bordered wheat image combines harvest plenty with garden delicacy in a deliberately rich sensory compound.
3: The twin-fawn simile for the breasts repeats 4:5 nearly verbatim: shenei shadayikh kishenei ofarim te'omei tseviyah (your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle). The near-verbatim repetition within a single book is a recognized feature of ancient Near Eastern poetry — not a scribal error but a structural device of deliberate echo. The twin gazelle image is attested in Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Harris 500, Song 2A) and in Ugaritic poetry for pairing of equals. Gazelle fawns were prized in the ancient Near East for their softness and the matching perfection of a twinned pair; the image captures both sensory gentleness and perfect symmetry. The deliberate internal echo between 4:5 and 7:3 creates a chiastic inclusio in the Song's central section, bracketing the wasf passages.
4: The geographical catalogue in 7:4 anchors the poem in specific, historically significant landscapes: Heshbon was the former Amorite royal city of King Sihon (Numbers 21:25-34), conquered by Israel in the Transjordanian campaign; the city and its pools are mentioned in Isaiah 16:8-9 as well. The pools of Heshbon (breichot be-cheshbon) at the gate of bat-rabbim (daughter of the multitude or daughter of many, H1337) were likely well-known irrigation features in the Transjordanian landscape. Damascus, the ancient capital of Aram/Syria, appears as a comparator for the nose — the prominent tower of Damascus toward Lebanon (migdal levanon tsofeh penei dammaseq) would have been recognizable to the audience as a landmark of the northern horizon. The ivory tower (migdal ha-shen) for the neck is the only ivory tower reference in the Hebrew Bible; ivory was an elite luxury material in the ancient Near East, associated with royal courts (1 Kings 22:39: Ahab's ivory house; Psalms 45:8: ivory palaces).
5: Ha-karmel (H3760) is both the specific place name (Mount Carmel, the prominent coastal promontory running into the Mediterranean at modern Haifa) and the common noun meaning a cultivated garden-land or fertile field. Carmel's distinctive feature in the landscape was its dense forests and orchards contrasting with the surrounding terrain — Isaiah 35:2 uses Carmel as an image of exceptional beauty and abundance. The hair like purple (velachur arganah) is the color of royalty — Tyrian purple, the most expensive dye of the ancient world, extracted from Murex sea snails along the Phoenician coast. The final clause — melekh asur ba-rehatim (the king is held/captivated in the flowing locks) — uses rahut (H7298, flowing channels/troughs or possibly streaming locks) to suggest that the beloved's hair has entrapped the king as channels capture flowing water. The image of hair as a net/trap for the beloved appears in Egyptian love poetry and in Proverbs 7:21 (seductress).
6: The direct-address appreciation — mah-yafet umah-na'amt ahavah bata'anugim (how fair and how pleasant you are, O love, among delights) — steps back from the anatomical inventory of the wasf to offer the holistic impression. Ta'anug (H8588, delight, pleasantness, luxury) is used of royal luxury in Micah 1:16 and of the delights of Qohelet's life project in Ecclesiastes 2:8. The plural bata'anugim (among/with all your delights) may be a genitive of attendant characteristic: love itself as the supreme delight. In Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Chester Beatty I), the holistic appreciation of the beloved — stepping back from the detailed description to exclaim about the whole — is a recognized formal transition in the wasf genre: detailed description followed by comprehensive appreciation is the standard pattern.
7: The date palm (tamar, H8558) was the economically dominant tree of the ancient Near East — the date palm was a symbol of Jericho (city of palms, Deuteronomy 34:3), of Mesopotamian abundance in Babylonian texts, and of beauty in Egyptian art. Palm trees were used as architectural motifs in Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:29, 32, 35) and in Ezekiel's vision of the restored temple (Ezekiel 40:16-31). In the ancient Near East, the date palm was one of the tallest, most graceful, and most productive trees in the agricultural landscape; a woman's stature compared to a date palm invokes all of these associations — height, grace, and fruitfulness. The breasts compared to date clusters (eshkolot, H811, clusters of grapes or dates) continues the harvest-abundance vocabulary that characterizes this wasf.
8: The declaration that the bridegroom will ascend the palm tree (e'eleh ba-tamar, I will ascend by the palm tree) describes an actual practice: date palm climbing for harvest was performed by skilled workers in the ancient Near East, climbing with a rope-and-foot technique to reach the date clusters at the crown. The date harvest was a major agricultural and commercial event — dates were pressed into blocks for trade, fermented into date wine, and stored as a caloric staple. The clusters/boughs (sansinav, H5577, its branches or fruiting stalks, a hapax in this form) were the primary commercial product. The comparison of the beloved's breath to apples (tapuchim, H8598, apples or quince) continues the orchard-garden sensory vocabulary of the Song's consistent landscape, and the comparison of her mouth to the best wine sets up the transition to v9b where the beloved's own voice takes over.
9: The mid-verse voice transition — the beloved takes up the speech in mid-sentence — is a distinctive feature of the Song's use of multiple voices and is noted by virtually all commentators. Holekh ledodi lemeyasharim dovev siftei yeshenim (going down smoothly for my beloved, flowing over the lips of those who are asleep) — the wine-flowing image for speech that flows sweetly and easily, even rousing the sleeping. The yeshenim (H3463, those who are asleep/sleeping ones) in connection with wine has been interpreted as the lips of the sleeping beloved murmuring to the bridegroom even in sleep, or as wine that revives even sleepers. Egyptian love poetry uses the image of wine-smooth speech as a standard compliment for the beloved's voice, connecting the sensory pleasures of speech and wine.
10: Teshuqah (H8669, desire, longing) is one of the most theologically significant words in the Song. It appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible: Genesis 3:16 (the post-fall curse on the woman: 'your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you'), Genesis 4:7 (sin crouching at the door with desire for Cain), and here in Song of Solomon 7:10. The Genesisoccurrences are both associated with distorted desire — the post-fall gender hierarchy and the desire of sin for the sinner. Song of Solomon 7:10 — ani ledodi ve'alai teshuqato (I am my beloved's and his desire is for me) — represents the positive redemption of the term: desire that is mutual, reciprocal, and uncoerced. This is the only occurrence of teshuqah in the Bible where the desire is straightforwardly positive and restored, suggesting that the Song participates in the redemption of what the fall corrupted in Genesis 3.
11: The invitation to go out into the fields and lodge in the villages (kefar, H3723, villages/hamlets — the plural of the very common agricultural settlement unit) is the beloved's initiative — she invites the bridegroom into her pastoral world. The rural pastoral setting contrasts with the urban tensions in Song of Solomon 3:1-5 and Song of Solomon 5:7 (city watchmen, urban night-search scenes). In the ancient Near East, the agricultural village was the basic social unit; the invitation to lodge in the village rather than the city palace suggests an egalitarian intimacy outside the formal structures of royal court life. Egyptian love poetry frequently uses the garden and the rural estate as the setting for erotic encounter, contrasting the freedom of the open countryside with the formal constraints of the house/city.
12: The spring vineyard inspection — nistrah hagefen pitach hasemadar heniczu ha-rimmonim (whether the vine has budded, whether the tender grape blossom has opened, whether the pomegranates have bloomed) — repeats the spring-survey vocabulary of 2:13 almost exactly (hagefanim semdar natenu reach, the vines in blossom give off fragrance; the pomegranates in bloom). The deliberate verbal echo creates a seasonal cycle: what was described as a sign of spring's arrival (Song of Solomon 2:13) is now proposed as an occasion for the beloved to give her love. The vineyard as the setting for erotic encounter was a recognized literary convention in Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Harris 500, Song 4: 'come, let us go to the field and make our bed in the garden'); the vineyard also carries its covenant connotations in the OT (Isaiah 5:1-7 uses the vineyard for YHWH and Israel).
13: Duda'im (H1736, mandrakes) are the Mandragora officinarum, a plant with fleshy fruit that grows in the Mediterranean region and has been associated with fertility and erotic stimulation in ancient Near Eastern popular belief. Mandrakes appear in Genesis 30:14-16, where Reuben finds them in the field during the wheat harvest and Rachel trades Jacob's company for Leah's mandrakes — a text that explicitly connects mandrakes with fertility desire. In ANE popular medicine, mandrakes were considered aphrodisiacs and fertility enhancers; the Akkadian term nam-tar-gira (love plant) may be a parallel. The 'new and old' stored fruits (chadashim gam-yeshanim tsafanti lecha, new things as well as old things I have stored up for you) suggests accumulated gifts across seasons — a long-term investment of love expressed in gathered fruit. The pattern of both new and old echoes the wisdom teacher's treasure in Matthew 13:52.
